CURRENT AFFAIRS.

An interesting Lx)m\edy on o phase of modern Trish life
could be written around the attempt to dump an unne-
cessary Englishman into the Clonmel Municipal Gas-

-works. A few years ago the attempt would have suc-
ceeded, but to-day it failed; the spirit of self-reliance
and Irish Ireland has asserted itself at last even in
charming *‘ tame’’ Clonmel. We are told, and we are
willing to believe it, that Clonmel is one of the most

- shoneen towns in Ireland. No doubt the Freemasons

are well and compactly organised there. Clonmel re-

cently made itself notorious by its begging—vain beg-.

ging, if we remember aright—for a detachment of
Tommies to help on its trade—and perhaps improve its
rals! The people probably are apathetic, and need
organised and stirred up, and so ‘‘ tames’’ are

dumped into the Corporation and other places, and the
“classy’’ folk, the Freemasons and the ** gintry’’ have
an easy job in working things after their own notions.

But the attempted importation into the Gasworks
has been thwarted. We understand that in the year
1824, or thereabouts, a man from Scotland, Mr. Knee-
shaw, was imported into Clonmel. The importation’s
son became gas manager, and the latter has now. re-
tired, or is about to retire. The present generation of
Kneeshaws are Ivish, and the grandson is apparently
by capacity and every other consideration suitable for
the vacant managership. But the grandson of a Scotch
importation is too Irish for some of those in charming,
““ tame’’ Clonmel, and it would have none of him: no
one less than a real live fresh importation—from Eng-
land this time—would suit the Gas Committee. It may
be mentioned that the Kneeshaws, though of Scotch
extraction, are now Catholics.

There was a long debate at the Clonmel Corporation
as to whether or not the public had a right to be pre-
sent at their meetings! Charming, dark Clonmel.
What a question even to debate at this time of day—
the right of the public to be present at a general meet-
ing of the Clonmel Corporation. Whilst the members
of the Corporation were debating the question, a body
of ratepayers—including some of the wealthiest men in
the town—practically solved the matter by opening the
door and walking in.

The Gas Committee had reported that they sought
he advice of their consulting engineer, an Englishman,
ne Isaac Carr, of Farnworth, Widnes, England, and
in the end the Gas Committee decided that a Mr. Bur:
on, of Stockport, was the most suitable candidate, and
they chose him to fill the position. The members of
the committee who signed this selection are Messts.
T. P. Morrissey, D. O’Connor, James Meehan, Thomas
Skehan, and James Cahill. Fortunately the projected
dump of the Gas Committee had to come before the
Corporation for ratification, and fortunately the public .
went into the meeting.

Alderman Morrissey stated that as long as he was a
member of the Council it was usual for the Council to
accept the report of the Gas Committee. Well, it was
none too soon that this Gas Committee got a set back.
Alderman Morrissey made an eloquent harangue into
which he dragged his conscience.  One. sentence is
enough for us. He said, ‘‘ In these matters they were
completely in the hands of their consulting engineer,"
their consulting engineer being a Mr. Carr, from Farn-
worth, Widnes, England! Alderman Morrissey said it
was a libel and an outrage to say that he and his col-
leagues on the Gas Committee would not give the pre-
ference to an Irishman. But the humbug of this re-
mark is plain from his previous statement that *“ In
these matters they were completely in the hands of
their consulting engineer.”  Alderman D. O’Connor
seconded Alderman Morrissey’s motion to dump a man
from Stockport into Clonmel over the heads of Trish
applicants. :

It appears that there were 20 applicants, eight of
whom were Irish born. There was a dispute as to &
question of fact whether or not three years ago the
Englishman, Mr. Carr, stated that the manager’s
son, Mr. Richard Kneeshaw-—who, by the way, might
be said to have been born, reared and lived his life in
the Gasworks—was fully competent to take, his father’s

place or not. Councillors Stapleton and Tobin, and
Councillor P. J. Condon were of opinion that he did
make the statement, but Mr. Carr denied any recol-
lection of it. Councillor Tobin, after Mr. Carr’s state-
ment, replied:—'‘I am not a member of the Council
for more than four years, and the statement was made
here in my presence. In reply to a question, Mr. Carr
said deliberately that Mr. Kneeshaw, jun., was fully
competent to take over the management, but that he
did not like to interfere with his father.” A previous
appointment made on the recommendation of Mr. Carr
was admitted to have not been successful, and that
seemed a rather delicate matter for those in favour of
dumping another Englishman on the recommendation
of Mr: Carr. -

Who is this Mr. Isaac Carr? Camnot the Clonmel
“Corporation get a consulting engineer in Ireland, and
why do they go to Farnworth, Widnes, England, for
one ?

Councillor Tobin was prepared to make an affidavit
that three years ago Mr. Carr said that young M.
Kneeshaw was qualified for the position. The Gas
Committee and their intended dump and their consult-
ing Mr. Carr were up against more than they bargained
for at this meeting of the Corporation, and the Mayor
began to feel the unexpected squeeze, for he said, ‘I
must say it is a reflection on the committee to be cri-
ticised in this way and called un-Irish, un-national and
anti-Clonmelmen.’’

Alderman Tom Condon, M.P., made a ponderous
speech as became a great statesman speaking at a local
Board, and supported the Gas Commiftee in their re-
commendation to dump an English gas manager on the
recommendation of the English consulting engineer,
Mr. Carr, into Clonmel. The result was, however, that
the gas committee’s dumping recommendation was
thrown out, notwithstanding that Alderman Tom Con-
don, M.P., threw in his weight with the dump. The
voting was as follows :—For the adoption of the report—
Alderman Condon, Alderman O’Connor, Alderman
Skehan, Alderman Cahill, Alderman Morrissey, and the
Mayor (6). Against—Councillors Burke, James O’Con-
nell, Tobin, Hickey, O’Connor, Halley, M. O’Connell,
P. J. Condon, O’Meara, Hogan, Farrell, Stapleton,
Magner, and Ryan (14). Councillor Roche declined to
vote. Further on we have some more notes about this
important question of the Irish gas industry.

Life is too short to read all the raucous and bitter
stuff and twaddle reported in the *“ Irish Times' of the
proceedings of the Presbyterians against Home Rule
at Belfast on Thursday of last week. We sampled the
speeches and read the ‘' Irish Times’ "’ leading article
on the affair. The ‘‘ Irish Times’’ commences by saying
—** We hope that thinking Nationalists will appreciate
the significance of yesterday’s Presbyterian demonstra-
tion against Home Rule.”  Well, it 1s seldom we agree
with the ‘‘ Irish Times,”” but we agree with it in this
matter, and we also hope that thinking Nationalists
will appreciate the matter fully. So far as we can see,
the attitude of the Presbyterians and of other sections
of the * Saved ” is that so long as a majority in this
country are Catholics, this country must not have Home
Rule. That is an attitude of great significance. If
these ranting anti-Catholics as a result of their endea-
vour to raise up a ** holy’’ war against Flome Rule suc-
ceed in preventing Home Rule, what wiil the * thinking
Nationalists,”” to use the phrase of the ‘‘ Irish Times,”’
have to conclude ?

Do the ranting anti-Catholics want to force on the
mind of the Nationalist Catholics that the latter cannot
get Home Rule so long as the former arve strong enough
to raise up a ““ holy’” anti-Catholic war in the United
Kingdom against the Catholics of Ireland ? The poor
““ tolerance provers’’ are getting it hard these days and
we have no sympathy for them. The so-called policy
of ** conciliation’” does not appear to have softened the
Presbyterians, Reformed or not as they may be.

Of course a lot of this Presbyterian talk is raimeis:
let a Catholic Nationalist tackle a Presbyterian with
whom he deals and we don’t suppose he will find much
of that sort of Belfast stuff on his lips, but plenty of
nice, soft, soothering talk, so as not to have the Catho-
lic account closed. But with all due discount for the
elements of raimeis and blatherskite in Presbyterian
anti-Catholic oratory we think the Presbyterians, who
get very good business support from Catholics and Na-
tionalists in Ireland are going a great deal too far, and
they may try the patience of their Catholic customers
over much. Even the tamest of Catholics must feel
some resenftment on reading of these Presbyterian and
TReformed Presbyterian outbursts of insensate anti-
Catholic bigotry.

The Right Hon. T. Sinclair, one of the Belfast law-
less persons, presided at one meeting, and spoke of
‘! their forefathers of Scottish Presbyterian blood,"”” who
were ““ planted "’ in Ulster three centuries ago. Ac-
cording to him, all the non-Catholic & re united in
this ““ holy”” war against Home Rule. He remarked :—

“ Our meeting is confined to Presbyterians, but
that is not from want of sympathy with any other
Protestant Church in Ireland. In working for our
own safety we are working for theirs as well. We

THE LEADER. .

are confident that they wish our gatherings to-day
the most cordial God-speed. We have all noticed
with great satisfaction that our Methodist brethren
purpose holding a meeting to-morrow with a view
of taking action in sympathy with our own, and on
Monday last I received the following letter from
the Primate of the Protestant Episcopal Church,
whose interest in the better inter-relations of the
Protestant denominations in Ireland is so well
known, and which I have great pleasure in read-
ing.”

The Rev. J. W. Gibson read out a statement about
an alleged boycott of a Mr. Farrington, a Presbyterian,
over a land transaction. But on the face of it if he is
boycotted it is because of the land transaction and not
because he is a Presbyterian: and again if he is boy-
cotted under the Union, what argument is that against
Home Rule ? But of course it is idle to expect thought
or logic from these anti-Home Rule Presbyterians whilst
they are inflamed with bigotry and anti-Irish and anti-
Catholic passions.

Who are the Reformed Presbyterians of Ireland?
At their Synod in Belfast a few days ago some tall reso-
lutions were unanimously passed. —The Synod con- .
demned Home Rule, and one of the reasons is that
Home Rule would make ‘‘ the man of sin,” which we
take it is the polite phrase in Reformed Presbyterians
of Treland Synods for referring to his Holiness the Pope
—it would make the ‘‘ man of sin’’ the ‘‘ real king, or
rather tyrant, of Ireland.”” Another reason why these
gentle and gentlemanly people of this Synod are against
Home Rule is that it would * swrrender the Protestant
minority in Ireland to an admittedly and ‘conscien-
tiously ” murderous ascendancy.”” So we poor Papists
are murderers as well as all the other dreadful things.
It is a wonder they accept us as customers. And these
are part of the people to whom the green ones of this
country go ‘‘ tolerance proving.”’

A resolution of this Synod of Reformed Presbyterians
of TIreland concerning Ne Temerc commences:—
“ Whereas the Pope of Rome cannot be recognised by
any Evangelical Christian as having any rightful autho-
rity over or in either Church or State, or as having any
such authority, even over his own blinded followers,
but is, and, in the judgment of this Synod, always has
been, the anti-Christian head of the anti-Christian
system commonly called the Church of Rome,” etc.
The italics are ours. Might we suggest that when these
Presbyterians ““ Reformed *’ themselves they forgot to
reform their manners. On the face of it, they must be
a pack of corner-boys judging from their impudent and
silly language. Towards the end of long-winded reso- -
lutions we read, ‘“ Synod further, on the subject of
mixed marriages, (1) enjoins the members of the church
not to contract such marriages with infidels, nor with
Papists, or other idolaters.”” What have the various
Presbyterians who enjoy Catholic business custom to
say to all this anti-Catholic blackguardism?

Look at him in his glorious prime!
Stalworth he’s since life’s work began ;
Travel about in any clime,
There’s to be found in freedom’s van,
Bearing its standard, grand, sublime,
The proud, undaunted Carrick man |

Never was known a friend so true !

So steadfast, leall And no one can
IBspy beneath skies gray or blue,

From Clareen Well to rapid Bann,
A heart that can both dare and do

For home more than the Carrick man |

See him beside the placid Suir,
Watch as he turns the hills to scan,

His eyes with love glow ardent, pure,
He’d suffer tortures rather than

Aught could away that feeling lure,
From pulse of him, the Carrick man |

The people trust his spoken word ;
He faithful is to kin and clan!
His soul’s emotions all are stirred
When he broods o’er the safest plan
“To lift veils which bright visions blurred,
Then valiant is the Carrick man!

His hand is open as the day,

Vice-like it grasps the friend’s who ran
With him along in danger’s way!

A tear falls when he thinks of Dan-
The host of others 'neath the clay,

Who loved were by the Carrick man.

‘Whether in comfort or in dearth,

On soil where sun’s rays burn and tan,
’Midst sob of grief or burst of mirth,

‘Where tempests roar or zephyrs fan,
Treland’s the dearest spot on earth—

It’s Eden to the Carrick man!

(REV.) JoHN A. JACEMAN.
Ord. -Min.

Brother and Rev. Chancellor O’Connor, M.A.,
D.G.C., speaking at the monthly service under the
_auspices of the City of Dublin Grand Orange Lodge in
10 Rutland Square, Dublin, said that the Protestant
minority ruled the world, and it was the prerogative of
Protestantism to govern! That should seftle us all.
Where is the use of us antiquated Papists expecting
to get any show in the higher reaches of Government,
railways, banks, insurance companies, and the rest
when it is ‘‘the prerogative of Protestantism to
govern ’’|

We have received from correspondents copies of the
Clonmel ‘‘ Nationalist ’’ and of the Clonmel “ Chron-
icle ” of last week. The Clonmmel ‘ Chronicle ’’ used
to be a Unionist paper; it is now, we understand, “‘in-
dependent.” We would like to know on whom does it
‘depend. We hear the editor is from Derry, where the
'Prentice Boys come from.

There is some unconscious humour in an item in the
“ Trish Times,”” headed ‘‘ Australian Help for Sir I.
Carson.”” The heading is to a telegram from Mel-
bourne announcing moral and material support. It is
signed ‘‘ SNOWBALL, Chairman ! Is it a hoax, we
wonder? ° If it isn’t, at least the pack of Australians
‘concerned might have appointed a chairman with a less
freezing name—some one with such a name as, say,

THE LEADER.

Fesruary 10, 1912.

Blood-Smith ; even Jenkins would be a great improve-
ment on Snowball.  Snowball could only be beaten
by Frost. Perhaps, it is all a joke and meant to sym-
bolise a thawing, snowball from Australia trickling
down the back of the disloyal ruffian, Carson.

The Lord Mayor of Belfast has sent a 1'eqmi'slﬁlon to HIF
military authorities for troops fm'_’l'h\lr?’(hy. I‘he”Belh\st
rcorrespondent of the ‘‘ Irish Times ™ says:— At all
events, the elaborate display of military force will dis-
prove at once the oft-repeated statement. of Radicals
and Nationalists that there is no opposition to Home
Rule in Belfast.”” In tho first place, as a matter of
fact, we have never heard that anyone ever stated that
there is no opposition to Home Rule in Belfast. As
for the presence of the military proving anything it will
certainly prove what none of us need to have proved,
that there is a rowdy corner-boy element in Belfast that
may break into riot on a favourable opportunity. In
Dublin, during the newsboys strike, some of the }mdcr-
world and corner-boys of Dublin broke out in rioting for
no explicable reason whatever, and partly terrorised
the city for some days, and extra forces of police had
to be drafted into Dublin.  So inexplicable was the
rioting that people were driven to the heab
then existing to attempt an explanation. Of
course, after the lawless conduct of such dis-
loyal ruffians as Lord Londonderry, Carson and the
rest, there must be some unrest amongst the corner-boy
class in Belfast at the present time, and we have no
doubt a show of force will be desirable to keep that
rabble in due subjection.

The sereeching sisterhood do not appear to have
taken much root in Ireland. When we get. Home Rule
will be the time to consider the question of votes for
women in Ireland. So far as we can judge Irish women
—real Irish women—are not to any extent anxious to
mix up in active politics, and look with amused con-
tempt on most of those few women in Ireland who, in
sympathy with the English screeching sisterhood, and,
as regards some of them, with an eye to earn cheap
notoriety for themselves, have taken up the matter
here. The screeching sisterhocd are an English im-
portation, and a section of the Anglicised natives imi-
tating England have taken up their cries just as Angli-
cised persons are keen to take up other English fads
and fancies. If Irish women who conduct business,
own property, and work for their living in the labour
market, want votes under Home Rule, they will, we
are sure, get them without any screeching. ~ But it is
time enough to think of that when we get Home Rule.
In the meantime we should look closely into the pos-
sible game of these Unionists and anti-Irish people who
see in this suffragette business a means of attacking the
Government pledged to carry Home Rule. One of the
importations, Miss Christabel Pankhurst, has been orat-
ing in Belfast, and said that no question, from her point
of view, could take precedence of the-question of votes
for women. Well from the Irish point of view Home
Rule is-the great matter, and votes for Irish women is
comparatively a small matter; and it will be dealt with
by a Home Rule Parliament. This votes for women is
so far mostly a bogus business in Ireland, and Ivish
women have not moved in the matter. That, so far as
16 exists here, it is an imitation of an English movement
is proved, to our satisfact’on anyway, by the fact that in
Treland it has not produced one woman leader capable
by ability and force of character to ‘stand out as its
champion. In order fo make any sort of a splash the
imitation suffragettes in Ireland have to import a Miss

The war on the penny dirtics goes on, It is not by
any means over yet. There is a law of the land, but
whether the law, or the people wha are supposed to
give eftect to it, or both, are to blame, the plain fact is
that 1 communities would be deluged with smut,
indecency and divt from England if they did not strike
out for themselves. A contributor in this issue gives
some interesting particulars of (he war on dirt in
Waterford.  The law of the land, which is law made in
England, is apparently incapable of dealing with this
pestilence.  England is-supposed to be Protestant,
but for the greater part it is nothing at all in the re-
ligious way; it has discarded Christianity, and is like a
once prosperous firm staving off the inevitable smash
by living on apital.  What we may call the momen-
tum of Christianity is not yet exhausted in England.
Ch i iven it some habits and traditions that
are a 3ut Ireland cannot afford to await
its moral marching orders from Kngland where the laws,
at present, come from; it must march itself to its own
tune.  In Waterford a priest had to strip a shop win-
dow of its picture postcar or some of them, and his
Lordship the Bishop has instructed his clergy to read
out the names of Catholic newsagents who break their
pledges not to sell dirty papers. The dirty evil calls
for such measures. English law is apparently incom-

We hear that on the part of the committee of the
Waterford Technical Institute there is a great tendency
to pander to the local snobocracy in connection with all
the public functions organised under the auspices of the
Institute. The public lectures were primarily intended
for students and people of the working classes, and at
first a nominal charge of 1d. or 2d. was made. But
that arrangement did not suit some of the upstarts, and
so the prices were raised to such a figure that the work-
ingmen were practically shut out, and now it looks as
if the students are to be squeezed out also. A letter
in the ** Waterford News'’ gives particulars of what
happened to at least some students of the JInstitute.
These students were told that no cheap tickets (3d. or
6d.) would be issued, that they must take 1s. tickets or
go away—no half-price or other concession for pupils.
They purchased 1s. tickets, and, on presenting them-
selves for admission, were rudely turned away and toltd
to go to the threepenny gallery. The students did as
they were bid—very foolishly we think—and were
packed away in the threepenny place, and they could
see other persons being passed into the shilling seats
and whole rows of reserved seats for which no one had
booked. When the students paid 1s. they should not
have consented to go into the threepenny place, but
probably the youngsters were overawed by the bully, or
bullies, concerned. =~ More ought to be heard of this
locally. :

A correspondent writes :—'‘ Regarding Clonmel and
gas. Many municipal authorities through Ireland have
technical schools, and also gas works, under their man-
agement. Why don’t these city fathers give encour-
agement to some. of the most promising students in the
chemistry class by giving free apprenticeships in the
gas works as prizes to their scholars, and in this manner
secure a sufficient supply of Irish gas managers, pro-
perly trained and qualified ? Of course this would take
some years to fructify, but it is the only way to make
a beginning.”’

This is a good suggestion : though we have yet to be
convinced that there are not sufficient Irishmen capable
of filling all the gas managerships that become vacant
from time to time. We believe there are strong forces
working against the chances of Irishmen in the gas in-
dustry. Freemasonry is, we have no doubt, one strong
force. In the case of Clonmel the consulting engineer,
who is an Englishman, has, or had, the whole matter
in his own hands, for, as one of the Gas Committee
said, ‘“In these matters they were completely in the
hands of their consulting engineer.” This consulting
engineer had previously recommended a man for a job
in the gas works at Clonmel, and the man turned out a
failure, yet even in face of that, when this Englishman
recommended another English dump, all the Gas Com-
mittee can say ‘is that they were completely in his
hands! Well, the Clonmel Corporation took the matter
out of the hands of this confessedly helpless Gras Com-
mittee, who were completely in the hands of their con-
sulting engineer—the only member of the Corporation
voting with the five committee men in favour of the
dump being Mr. Tom Condon, M.P. The committee
have since resigned in a body. This, we suppose, was
the only course left open to them, for the defeat of their
proposed dump was a vote of want of confidence in
them. The Corporation of Clonmel will have no diffi-
culty in appointing another Gas Committee. There is
a firm of gas engineers in Westminster, England, that
have a big hold on portion of the gas industry in Ire-
land, and thaf, no doubt, is another force making for
the difficulty of manning Trish gas works with Irishmen.

When an industry is imported into a country like
Ireland it is possible by good organisation to keep it
largely out of the hands of the natives. If the natives
get only a partial chance to learn the business ade-
quately, it will be necessary in many cases to import
men from the other side as higher positions become
vacant. Something like this has apparently happened
in the gas industry in Ireland. We know little or no-
thing about gas, but on the face of it the gas industry

is not such a wonderful thing that, given a fair oppor- .
tunity, Ireland could not produce as mally gas manag‘ers
and experts as she required after a certain number of

years. And the gas industry is an old one in Ireland, -

and there has not only been time, but time ten times
over, for the natives to learn the industry if foreign
forces and pernicious- fayouritism, with a bit of the
square and compass, had not been at work thwarting
the play of what we may call natural forces in the
matter.

The suggestion of our correspondent is & very good
one, and we commend it to the careful consideration of
all municipal authorities who also control gas works and
technical schools. In the meantime Irish managers for
Trish gas works should be the cry, and a man should not

be imported until it was clearly and openly shown and .

demonstrated that the importation was on business
principles unavoidable.

The gas managers in Ireland have an association, but
so far as we know we think it is rather an association of
gas managers in Ireland—who are largely if not mostly
imported—and not an association of Irish gas managers.
We think the Irish gas managers—men born in Ireland
—ought to have an association of their own and put up
a fight for the interests of Irishmen in the industry. They
can count on us to give them every backing and support
in reason.

The Mohill tolerance proving case is yet exciting much
interest in the locality. What has Smyth, M.P.,

the ‘‘tames.”” The successful candidate, according

Board of Guardians and received promises of support
from many of them, there being no other candidate at
the time in the field. This correspondent wants to
know if we would wish the Guardians to break their
promise. Well, we would say that the ‘‘ tame *> Guar-
dians had no business in giving a promise; they should
have waited until they saw what candidates were com-
peting for the post.

Some of his constituents are not taking kindly to
Mr. T. F. Smyth over this matter. On a recent Sun-
day he went to the Aughavas part of his constituency to
talk to the people. The priests of the parish ignored
him, and the people, we understand, did not want to
hear him. By the way, are the Mohill Guardians going
to insist on their new doctor living in his dispensary
district, as set down by the regulations of the Local
Government Board in these matters? We understand
Rowan is not the best place in Treland for the residence
of a medical man. The district is poor and the private
practice in it would not be worth much. Notwith-
standing this, the Guardians have insisted upon their
medical officer living in the place.  Within a short
period of time three medical men have left the ‘district.
These men were Papists. Now that Rowan has a
non-Papist doctor, will it insist that he must live in the
district?  Time will tell, and we will watch.

POLITICS IN DALKEY.
THE DALKEY RATEPAYERS’ ASSOCIATION.

'ONSIDERING the large circulation the LEADER has

in Dalkey, I propos 4 a few articles for

the instruction of the Nationalists and Catholics of the

township on the political tactics of their enemies, the
Conservatives and Protestants.

The Conservatives, who are in a minority of about
fifty or sixty on the register, know very well that in a
straight fight at an election, Conservative v. Nationalist,
they could not hope to win a single seat. They are a
shrewd, a cunning and an ambitious body, and they
crave and almost wither away with longing for power on
the Council. They recognise that the only chance they
have of attaining municipal power is by trying to hide
or obscure their Toryism under the non-political cloak.
They cry, we want no politics and no religion on the
Council and in the management of the Council’s affairs;
we want straight business and efficient and economic
administration—the suggestion being that Nationalists,
of course, want nothing of the kind!

‘“ Straight business,’” says the Tories, ‘“‘may win for us
but straight politics won’t.  So let us, in the name of
holy Martin Luther, submerge our Toryism, and try,
with the help of dear old Leckey, to split the Nationalist
vote.”” And in order to put this policy into action they
form what they call the Dalkey Ratepayers: Associa-
tion. It is with this Association T propose to deal in this
article.

The ‘‘ Dalkey Ratepayers' Association '’ is a body
composed for the most part of quiet, steady, plodding,
successful business-men, the majority of whom are Pro-
testants and Conservatives, and a large proportion are
Masons.  Whether any of the Catholic members are
Masons or not I have not been able so far to discover.

. On paper the constitution of the association is strictly
non-political and non-sectarian, i.e., it professes to
have nothing whatever to do with politics or religion.

lord with it, and becomes for a fixed period the tenant
of the State. - During this period he pays, in lieu of
rent, an annuity, which represents both interest g,nd
sinking fund on the capital sum adyanced to him.

But just as all seemed satisfactorily settled, and the

on finance.  The purchase money was raised by the
issue of 2% per cent. Guaranteed Land Stock. This
Stock, like all Government securities at that period,
fell heavily from the first. The Act seems to have
been based upon the assumption that Stock could be,
and would be, raised at par value. The assumption
was altogether false. The loss in flotation fell on the
Trish Development Grant (no part of which is used for
Development), and when that was exhausted on the
Irish ratepayer through deductions from the grants in
aid of Local Taxation. In 1908 things came to a
crisis, and further issues of Stock would have entailed
a heavy loss out of Irish rates. Land purchase had to
be stopped, or a way found out of the impasse. This
state of things led to Mr. Birrell’s Land Act of 1909,
which was based upon the Report of a Treasury Com-
mittee. This Act reverted to the old policy of pay-
ment in stock (8 per cent.), the tenants’ annuity was
raised from 3} to 3% per cent., the period of redemp-
tion reduced from 68} years to 65% years, and the
landlords’ bonus allocated on a sliding scale under
which the higher the price the lower the bonus. The
losses borne by the Irish ratepayer under the Act of
1903 are now borne by the Imperial Exchequer.
Powers of compulsory sale are given to the Congested
Districts Board and the Estates Commissioners. The
Act was considerably altered for the worst in the

and is working slowly but satisfactorily since. It
was absolutely necessary. owing to a cause beyond
humq-u cont-rol', the condition of the money market.

had 1o new clement entered into the struggle, it is pos-
sible that another three hundred years might have seen
Treland entirely Anglicised. i

But even in the passing of three centuries, England
had not learnt what it was that lay closest to the heart
of the neighbour whom she was vainly frying to per-
suade or coerce into becoming her sister or her daughter.

In the reign of Henry VIII. the so-called Reforma-
tion took place, and very soon the reformers, who had
found the pillaging of churches and of monasteries in
their own counfry so profitable, turned their attention
to the rich ccclesiastical foundations of Ireland.

. By this time the descendants of many of the first
planters, although living more or less apart from the
original inhabitants, were almost more Trish than were
the Irish themselves. They were Catholics, as all
England had been when their ancestors had accepted
the confiscated lands of banished Irish nobles, and now
by preference they chose Irish names for their children,
and, whilst habitually speaking the language of the
Gael, they were often without any knowledge of the
Anglo-Saxon tongue.  In spite of all this, they had
naturally accepted, more readily than those of purely
Milesian descent, the ascendancy of the English, but
now something occurred which at one stroke severed
all Irish Catholics from any sympathy with England,
and bound them together as nothing before had ever
done. 3

The veformed religion was suddenly launched in
Ireland, and orders were issued that everyone must
conform. Not even then did England realize what she
was doing.  The history of the English martyrs testi-
fies to the resistance to death made in England itself
against the claim of Henry VIII. to be the head of the
Church and against the other laws of the ‘‘ Reform."
But despite the heroic resistance of the minority, even
before the end: of that one reign, that majority had
adopted the tenets of the new heresy. It remained for
Elizabeth and James I. to continue the persecution of
Catholics, yet the ultimate fate of the Catholic Church
in England was practically decided within a few years
of King Hen granting to himself the divorce from
Queen Katharine, which the Pope had refused.

It was thought, therefore, that in Treland, the
poorer, less populated and often divided country, the
reformed Church would also speedily be victorious, and
it tcok not years, but centuries of persecutions to show
England that, whatever else an Irishman may sur-
render: fortune, properby, personal liberty and even
life itself, he will not give up the faith which for
him is the passport into Heaven. Confiscations, per-
secutions even to death, coercion, tyranny and oppres-
sion, everything was tried to.draw Ireland from her
allegiance to the Church of God, and gradually it
dawned upon the reforming party that so long as the
Irish continued to use their own language, they would
never falter in their attachmenf and their loyalty to
the Catholic religion.

Turning to the pages of Paropeaca na nDaoine, one
realizes to some extent the part that religion occupied

in the lives of those from whom it was now decided it
must at any cost be wrested. To begin with the first
hours of the day, there are prayers handed down to us
that were to be said on waking, others on rising and
when risen, and there is even a prayer for the house-
wife to say when she is making a bed. Other daily
duties, too, have their commendations to God. There
are prayers when malking bread, \vlm.n putting a child
to sleep, when going out and coming in; prayers before
and after speaking; then, when the day is done, a
prayer when covering the glowing turf, when in bed,
when going to sleep and when waking in the night.
But it was not only the life of the Bean an Tighe that
was interwoven with a chain of prayer, the compiler of
Paropeaca na n'Osome has helped to hand down from
the long past years prayers that apply to men as much
as to women. Indeed, the sailor, the shepherd, the
fisherman and others have their own prayers in addi-
tion to those they hold in common with the other
members of their family.

Iiven the daily greetings of the Trish were an offence
in the ears of those who had spent three hundred years
in trying unsuccessfully to liken the Celtic nature to the
Saxon.

To the fanatical reformers who allowed no honour to
the Mother of God or to the saints, the Gaelic greet-
ings, “ God and Mary with you,”” and the answer,
““God and Mary and also Patrick,”” or Bridget, or
Columbeille, or Declan, were unendurable, and scar-
cely less so were the words addressed to all workers:
““ God bless the work,’” or those spoken on entering a
house: ‘‘ God bless all here.”

The only way to put a stop to this continual remem-
brance and praise of God was to uprool the language
which teemed with such prayers.  The use of the
Gaelic tongue had long been discouraged because of the
acilities its use was supposed to afford for plottings and
for treason, but it was the fanatical hatred of the re-
formers towards the religion with which.it was en-
twined that sounded its death-knell.

It was only when the work of language destruction

rad outwardly been completed that those who had the
real good, or—if it is allowable so to express oneself—
the beautiful good, of their country at heart, realized
the full extent of the wrong done.
The promise of Saint Patrick that Ireland would re-
main faithful to Supernatural Truth had not been'
broken despite all the power behind the Protestant as-
cendancy ; overt persecution, penal laws, and again re:
peated plantations with the petty and covert persecu-
tions entailed thereby, had not changed the belief of the
bulk of the Irish people, for the members of the re-
formed Church in Ireland are, with few exceptions, of
Inglish or of Scofitish extraction.

But the eradicating of the Irish language had suc-
ceeded in doing something that was only less evil than
the object which it had been intended to accomplish.
In taking away from the suffering, struggling people
the language-bond which, in spite of subjugation, had
kept it a nation still, it had destroyed in the Irish the
pride of country which is the safeguard and support of
true nationality. Not the pride of race, that is deep
and strong in every Irish heart; not the love of coun-
try, for that is as firm to-day as it was before Danish
invader or Norman conqueror ever crossed the western

the United States were on the decrease. Being a
member of the Sinn Fein Society of New York, and a
believer in Irish industry, I took upon myself with
others to try and encourage the sale of Irish linens.

York, and found that in almost every case spurious goods
were being sold to the public. One firm (the principal
stockholder an English baronet) were selling Syrian
laces made in New York as real Irish hand-made cro-
chet. By pretending to be very green, I found out that
the lace was bought by weight. I took a stroll through
the store (a firm employing over 2,000) and discovered

HOUSE OF MERCY.

Under the Care of the Sisters of Meroy, Baggot Street.

The above Institution is devoted to the care and protec-
tion of Destitute Young Girls of good character, whose youth
and inexperience render their position in the world one of
peculiar danger.

Its object being to afford IMMEDIATE Protection to those
to whom DELAY often means RUIN, these Young Girls
are admitted - without ANY CHARGE whatever, and
THOUSANDS of such have been received and provided for
since its establishment in 1831, being thus rescued from
misery and destitution through the charitable support
hitherto given to the Institute.

The House of Mercy constantly shelters and maintains
over 40 Young Girls, who are trained as Laundresses and
Domestic Servants, and thus equipped to become useful and
self-supporting members of society. A

The Sisters of Mercy now confidently appeal to the gene-
rosity of the public to enable them to carry on the good work,
as the Institution has no Government grant or aid, and is
perhaps less lknown than most other charities.

SICK AND DYING POOR
(Visited and Relieved by the Sisters of Meroy).

The Sisters make annually about 1,300 visits to the Sick
Poor in some of the most wretched localities in this large
city. Numerous heartrending and touching cases of real
misery and distress come thus constantly before them, re-
quiring timely and generous aid, which, if they are enabled
to administer, tonds to bring the poor sufferers safely
through a season of great privation and danger. S

Q

Tho Sisters feel assured an appeal in favour of tho above

great works of mercy will meet with a truly generous response
from the Benevolent, who will thus assist in enabling the
Sisters to continue their labours for the comfort and well-
being of God’s poor. >

DOWNPATRICK UNION.

Matron Wanted.

The Board of Guardians of above Union will, at their

meeting on Saturday, the 10th February, proceed to
appoint a competent person to act as Matron of the
Workhouse, at a salary of £80 per year with first class
rations and apartments.

In addition to this salary a sum of £12 10s. per annum
will be allowed to the Matron as remuneration for the
care of the Workhouse children.

The person who may be appointed will be held re-
sponsible for the cutting out, and to give the necessary
directions for making and mending the linen of all the
inmates and other clothing of the female inmates and
children. She will be also required to carry out the
duties of Matron as contained in Article 65 of the Work-
house Rules.

Applications, accompanied by testimonials and nam-
ing two solvent sureties willing to enter into a joint
bond for the sum of £100, will be received by me up to
8 o’clock a.m. on above date, when candidates will re-
quire to be in attendance.

By Order, R. L. MORROW, Clerk of Union.

Clerk’s Office, Down Union, 31st January, 1912.

DOWNPATRICK UNION.

Master Wanted,

The Board of Guardians of above Union will, at their
meeting to be held on Saturday, the 10th February, at
12 o’clock, proceed to appoint a competent person to
act as Master of the Workhouse at a salary of £60 per
year with first class rations and apartments.

The person ‘who may be appointed will be expected
to discharge all the duties required by the General
Regulations and Orders of the Local Government Board.

Applications, accompanied by testimonials as to
character and competency, and containing the names
of two solvent sureties, willing to enter into a joint bond
for the sum of £200, will be received by me up to
8 a.m. on above date, when candidates will be required
to be in attendance.

A By Order,
R. L. MORROW,
Clerk of Union.
